title: Kotlety Mielone
subtitle: Polish minced pork cutlets
excerpt: Polish home-cooked pork patties — mince mixed with grated onion, egg, and a little allspice, shaped and pan-fried until golden. Served with mashed potato and sauerkraut.
sourceNotes: Kotlety mielone (ground meat cutlets) are the most common home-cooked meat dish in Poland, found in every household cookbook. Documented in Maria Lemnis and Henryk Vitry, Old Polish Traditions in the Kitchen and at the Table (1979).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked and cooled for up to 3 months. Reheat in a frying pan with a little butter.

content.text: Kotlety mielone are the pan-fried pork mince patties of the Polish kitchen — similar in form to a hamburger but the seasoning takes a different direction. The onion is grated rather than chopped (it disappears into the mince and keeps it moist), and a little ground allspice gives a warm, slightly aromatic background note that places the dish firmly in the Polish kitchen.

The patties are coated in breadcrumbs before frying, which gives a light crust without the heavier coating of a schnitzel. They should be cooked through completely — pink pork is not a tradition here. A good dark crust on the outside means they are ready.

items.prepNote: soaked in 3 tbsp milk for 5 minutes, then squeezed

content.text: Combine the pork mince, grated onion, egg, squeezed breadcrumbs, allspice, salt, and pepper. Mix thoroughly with your hands until the mixture is uniform. With wet hands, divide into 8 equal portions and shape each into a flat oval patty, about 1.5 cm thick. Dust each patty lightly in flour, then press firmly into the breadcrumbs to coat on both sides.

Melt the lard in a large frying pan over medium heat. Fry the patties in batches for 4 to 5 minutes per side until deep golden brown and cooked through — cut one open to check if unsure; there should be no pink. Do not move them too early or the crust won't set. Serve with mashed potato, sauerkraut, and a spoonful of the pan drippings over the top.

items.symptom: Patties fall apart in the pan
items.cause: Mixture too wet or not mixed enough
items.fix: Squeeze the soaked breadcrumbs very dry before adding; mix the meat thoroughly so the protein binds

items.cause: Not enough flour under the breadcrumbs
items.fix: Dust generously in flour first; the flour is the glue that holds the breadcrumbs

content.text: Kotlety mielone are Polish school dinners, Sunday lunches, and weeknight suppers simultaneously — a dish that appears on every table regardless of region or generation. They are served with ziemniaki (boiled or mashed potatoes) and a salad of grated carrot, beetroot, or cucumber in sour cream. The version with half beef and half pork is more common in city cooking; countryside versions often use pork alone.
